Okay, I just want to go through one more example of the moment of highest tension. The reason I want to show Greg Hickman's here is because it's just a great example of what we spoke about in the 67 Golden Questions about using psychographics to bring your copy to life. Now, a lot of people will see this section and think that the language is really colorful, really specific, and how do you know what to use, what books to reference, what music to reference, what products to reference, and all that comes back down to those psychographics. So this is how it plays out in the real world within the context of the moment of highest tension. So, headline, we've reached a funnel implementation crisis and damn right, it's not your fault. Let me know if this sounds familiar. So that is a line you can use to open up your moment of highest tension whenever you feel stuck and you're about to really present this vivid three-dimensional experience that reflects exactly what they've been through before. So let me know if this sounds familiar. You started this whole funnel building thing with a fully loaded Starbucks card and a full tank of we're going to make it rain motivation. We bought into the proven systems, 12-step blueprints and magic funnel on steroids formulas invested in the premium plans of all the go to software's lead pages, click funnels, sent card, etc. Seeing the extra investment as a drop in the bucket compared to what your funnel will be poised to bring in. So really matching the inner experience of that moment of highest tension pillaged your network to line up a dream team of freelancers, designers, copywriters, programmers, automation people, Facebook ads, people's VA's, etc. And then you queued up the productive morning playlist on Spotify and got to work. Very specific there. You are ready to make your dent primed to be the success story that other people talked about instead of the one that convinced you to buy that last program. Hell no, you weren't motivated by Pina Coladas on a beach or a 600 horsepower Lambo, you'd be too nervous to drive around the block. So there's a shout out to the Ethos Graphics from the 67 Questions Workbook. Honestly, you'd be totally cool with the luxury to enjoy an extended morning routine complete with buttered coffee, Beethoven, and the best seller that Amazon drone shipped to your door last week that you haven't even cracked open. All while your sales roll in and customers get served on autopilot. But things haven't quite worked out that way. Your hashtag miracle mornings have turned into midday meltdowns. Your killer software stack sits unused with every subsequent rebuild and nasty reminder of how little momentum you've generated. Once again, a very true to life experience. And as launch my funnel headlines your quarterly goals for the eighth straight time, even you, the eternal freak and optimist are starting to doubt it will ever actually happen. That you'll get to grab a juicy piece of this whole freedom thing the internet's been going apeshit over for the last 10 years. And that you could actually be the calm, powerful and profitable CEO of a fully leveraged and quickly scaling online business instead of constantly being air dropped into a marketing battlefield filled with contradictory advice, shiny objects set to stun and more gurus than an Indian ashram. So this example really dives into the specificity of language. A lot of the things we got while doing the 67 questions workbook and then using that type of language to mirror their day to day experience of both having the optimism around achieving the goal and being disillusioned. And of course, all the ways that that disillusionment then appears in their day to day life. Once again, with hyper specificity. So with all that said, time to write your moment of highest tension and I will catch you in the next lesson where we lighten things up with the moment of highest pleasure. I'll see you there.
